brief_document.title: Establish liminal crate with foundation types and error taxonomy
brief_document.purpose: Bootstrap the liminal crate with Cargo.toml, the public API surface in lib.rs, the foundational channel types (ChannelMode, ChannelConfig, ChannelHandle), and the error taxonomy. This is the leaf dependency for every other brief in the cluster.
brief_document.task: Create the liminal crate from scratch. Define the core types that everything else depends on: ChannelMode (Ephemeral/Durable), ChannelConfig (name, schema, mode — no silent defaults per P6), ChannelHandle (the caller-facing handle for publish/subscribe/close). Define LiminalError covering channel, conversation, schema, and delivery failure cases, implementing std::error::Error and Debug. Set up lib.rs to re-export the channel, conversation, envelope, and error modules. This brief creates the skeleton — channel actor behaviour (LIM-002), schema validation (LIM-003), and conversation actor (LIM-004) build on it.

requirements.title: Create crate manifest with beamr dependency
requirements.spec: crates/liminal/Cargo.toml SHALL declare the liminal crate with beamr as a dependency. The crate SHALL use the workspace edition, rust-version, and lint settings. The crate SHALL NOT depend on haematite, aion, or any network library — those are separate clusters.
acceptance: Cargo.toml exists at crates/liminal/Cargo.toml with [package] name = "liminal"
beamr appears in [dependencies] with features = ["json"]
Workspace lints inherited via [lints] workspace = true
No dependency on haematite, aion, tokio, axum, or tonic

requirements.title: Define ChannelMode enum
requirements.spec: ChannelMode SHALL have exactly two variants: Ephemeral (in-memory only, no persistence) and Durable (backed by haematite, survives restart). The enum SHALL derive Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq.
acceptance: ChannelMode::Ephemeral and ChannelMode::Durable are the only variants
ChannelMode derives Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq
ChannelMode is re-exported from lib.rs

requirements.title: Define ChannelConfig requiring explicit name, schema, and mode
requirements.spec: ChannelConfig SHALL require a channel name (String), a schema definition (SchemaRef — opaque reference, defined fully in LIM-003), and a ChannelMode. ChannelConfig SHALL NOT implement Default — every field is required, no silent defaults (P6). A builder pattern MAY be used if it enforces all-fields-required.
acceptance: ChannelConfig has fields: name (String), schema (SchemaRef), mode (ChannelMode)
ChannelConfig does NOT implement Default
ChannelConfig::new(name, schema, mode) constructor requires all three arguments
SchemaRef is a placeholder type (unit struct or newtype) — LIM-003 fills in the real definition

requirements.title: Define ChannelHandle with publish, subscribe, and close
requirements.spec: ChannelHandle SHALL expose publish, subscribe, and close methods as the public API for interacting with a channel. publish SHALL accept a message payload and return a Result. subscribe SHALL return a SubscriptionHandle. close SHALL signal the channel actor to shut down gracefully. ChannelHandle SHALL be Clone and Send + Sync (it is a handle to a process, not the process itself).
acceptance: ChannelHandle has publish(&self, payload) -> Result<(), LiminalError> method signature
ChannelHandle has subscribe(&self) -> Result<SubscriptionHandle, LiminalError> method signature
ChannelHandle has close(&self) -> Result<(), LiminalError> method signature
ChannelHandle is Clone + Send + Sync
SubscriptionHandle is defined as a stub type in channel/subscription.rs

requirements.title: Define LiminalError enum with thiserror
requirements.spec: LiminalError SHALL cover: ChannelNotFound, ChannelClosed, SchemaMismatch, PublishFailed, SubscriptionFailed, ConversationFailed, ConversationTimeout, ParticipantCrashed, DeliveryFailed. Each variant SHALL carry a descriptive message string. LiminalError SHALL implement std::error::Error via thiserror and Debug.
acceptance: LiminalError is defined in crates/liminal/src/error.rs
All nine variants listed are present with message fields
LiminalError derives Debug and uses #[derive(thiserror::Error)]
LiminalError is re-exported from lib.rs

requirements.title: Set up lib.rs with module declarations and re-exports
requirements.spec: lib.rs SHALL declare pub mod for channel, conversation, envelope, error, and causal modules. lib.rs SHALL re-export the primary public types: ChannelMode, ChannelConfig, ChannelHandle, LiminalError. lib.rs SHALL contain ONLY module declarations and re-exports — no logic (CN3).
acceptance: lib.rs declares pub mod channel, conversation, envelope, error, causal
lib.rs re-exports ChannelMode, ChannelConfig, ChannelHandle, LiminalError
lib.rs contains zero function definitions, zero trait definitions, zero struct definitions
crates/liminal/src/channel/mod.rs exists with pub mod types, subscription
crates/liminal/src/conversation/mod.rs exists as a placeholder (populated by LIM-004)
cargo check -p liminal passes

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement channel actor behaviour — that is LIM-002.
SHALL NOT implement schema validation logic — that is LIM-003.
SHALL NOT implement conversation actor — that is LIM-004.
SHALL NOT add any persistence or haematite integration — those are durability cluster briefs.
SHALL NOT add any network or wire protocol code — that is the protocol cluster.
verification: cargo clippy -p liminal -- -D warnings passes clean.
lib.rs contains only pub mod and pub use — no logic.

adrs.title: Conversations as the fundamental messaging unit, not messages
adrs.decision: The fundamental unit is the conversation — a supervised beamr process owning a structured exchange between parties — over the message. A conversation mediates lifecycle, failure handling, and (optionally) durability. Exchange patterns (request-reply, streaming, saga) are conversation types with different lifecycle rules, not special-case bolt-ons. The rejected alternative is the NATS/Kafka model: messages as the primitive, patterns assembled from messages.

adrs.title: Embedded-first architecture with standalone mode as a deployment option
adrs.decision: The bus is a Rust library (crate) first, a standalone binary second. The embedded story (Aion workflows and bus channels in the same beamr instance) is the primary use case. Standalone mode is the same code with a network listener. The rejected alternative is standalone-first (adds a deployment dependency and network hop for every message, negating the beamr advantage).

checklist.text: crates/liminal/Cargo.toml exists with beamr as a dependency.
crates/liminal/src/lib.rs re-exports channel, conversation, envelope, and error modules.
ChannelMode enum has Ephemeral and Durable variants.
ChannelConfig requires name, schema, and mode — no silent defaults.
ChannelHandle provides publish, subscribe, and close operations.
LiminalError enum covers channel, conversation, schema, and delivery failure cases.

stories.text: As an application developer, I want to create a typed channel with a single function call so that I can start publishing and subscribing without configuring infrastructure.

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. Extract into submodules if approaching the limit.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error.
Schema validation rejects invalid messages at publish time — a subscriber never receives a message that does not match its declared schema.
Participant crash detection is via beamr process linking — no heartbeat polling, no timeout-based detection.
Ephemeral channels have zero persistence overhead — no haematite calls, no disk I/O, no serialisation beyond message delivery.
Channel and conversation actors are supervised. Crash restarts the individual actor without affecting other channels or conversations.

resolved_context.intention: Liminal's core is a messaging system where conversations replace messages as the fundamental unit. When this cluster is complete, a developer can create typed channels, publish messages with schema validation at publish time, subscribe with pattern matching, and initiate supervised conversations that own the state of a structured exchange. The bus participates in processing — it knows when consumers crash, mediates backpressure, and can resume durable conversations from where they left off. The experience should feel like writing function calls between processes, not configuring infrastructure.
